# Tesla enlists startups to fix its troubled 4680 battery line

Inviting startups into a live cell line is a tacit admission that Tesla's in-house fix for the 4680 has fallen far short of what it needed to do. By Stewart Burnett

* By  [Stewart Burnett](https://www.automotiveworld.com/author/stewart-burnettautomotiveworld-com/)

Tesla has launched a “Cell Giga Challenge” at Gigafactory Berlin, opening its live 4680 battery cell production line to external startups tasked with fixing bottlenecks in materials, automation, equipment, operations and AI. The programme, run with Berlin-Brandenburg incubator JUNI, is open for applications until 24 July, and aims to address longstanding problems that have dogged the 4680 cell for years.

Applicants must show a working proof of concept that is genuinely relevant to the manufacturing process and scalable, progressing through five stages: online application, screening, technical discussion, a pitch day, and pilot talks with Tesla’s cell team. Tesla says it is seeking solutions that “measurably improve quality, speed, cost, safety, or scalability”. The strongest teams will progress to a paid pilot working inside the Grünheide plant from August onwards.

Opening a live factory floor to outside startups is an unusual move for any global automaker, let alone one with Tesla’s historically guarded approach to its business secrets. The automaker is already unique in that it [redacts](https://www.automotiveworld.com/articles/tesla-logs-three-robotaxi-crashes-in-one-day-withholds-info/) much of the information in its regulator reports regarding self-driving crashes. In this case, opening the floor to startups arguably serves as a tacit admission that solving the 4680’s outstanding cost and yield problems in-house has not gone far enough. 

Still, Tesla is simultaneously doubling down on its volume targets for the cell. It has committed a further US$250m to lift Giga Berlin’s cell target from 8 GWh to 18 GWh, pushing cumulative investment in the cell unit toward around US$1.2bn. The automaker has separately confirmed plans in June to [raise vehicle output](https://www.automotiveworld.com/news/teslas-giga-berlin-reportedly-running-at-40-capacity/) and add 1,000 jobs, its second 20% production increase this year. 

Whether that counts as the expansion Chief Executive Elon Musk dangled before workers in the weeks preceding Grünheide’s works council election is unclear. Musk had warned employees in a pre-recorded video that future plans, including potential Cybercab and Semi production, would not proceed if the plant fell under stronger union influence. Among his video remarks to workers, he remarked: “we will not close the factory, but realistically we will also not expand”.

That vote saw IG Metall’s [works council share fall](https://www.automotiveworld.com/news/teslas-berlin-giga-rejects-ig-metall-in-works-council-vote/) from 39.4% to 31.1%, leaving Grünheide the only automotive plant in Germany without a collective bargaining agreement, a status Tesla has aggressively defended since the site opened in 2022. The union needed 19 of 27 seats; its share actually fell to just 13. 

IG Metall has separately alleged aggressive management tactics, including unannounced home visits to absent staff and wage withholding used to pressure termination agreements Separate claims emerged in the run-up to the vote, that an IG Metall worker had illegally recorded a meeting with management, resulted in a [defamation dispute](https://www.automotiveworld.com/news/ig-metall-accuses-tesla-factory-manager-of-defamation/) between the union and Plant Manager André Thierig.

The cell at the centre of the new challenge remains Tesla’s most troubled manufacturing programme. Independent testing has found 4680 cells built at Tesla’s Austin plant deliver around 244 Wh/kg of energy density, roughly 13% below the 269 Wh/kg of the Panasonic 2170 cells they were meant to replace. Musk eventually conceded at Tesla’s 2025 shareholder meeting that the pack’s dry electrode manufacturing process had proven “way harder” than anticipated.

Panasonic, Tesla’s main external 4680 supplier, [recently delayed](https://www.automotiveworld.com/news/panasonics-4680-mass-production-pushed-back-yet-again/) mass production of its own version of the cell for a second time, still waiting on a purchase order from a customer people familiar with the matter say is likely Tesla. Production lines at its Wakayama plant, originally slated to start in March 2024, have missed every deadline since. South Korean cathode supplier L&F has separately written down a US$2.9bn Tesla supply contract to a paltry US$7,386, citing the collapse in demand tied to the programme.

The cell’s weak performance has had commercial consequences beyond the supply chain: Cybertruck sales, which the 4680 was meant to support at 250,000–500,000 units annually, remain in the low five figures two years after launch. Buyers of the sole European Model Y trim using the 4680 pack have also reported cancellations after finding it delivers less range than the LG cell it replaced. 

Against that backdrop, inviting outside startups to help fix the line looks less like an open-innovation gesture and more like an acknowledgment that Tesla’s in-house effort has run out of easy answers.
